Medieval Latin Scholasticism

Henry of Ghent

c. 1217 – 1293

A secular master on divine illumination, intellect, and the self.

Henry of Ghent does not give us a single treatise called “On Mind,” and he does not use “mind” in the post-Cartesian sense. A philosophy of mind has to be reconstructed from his treatments of soul, intellect, will, knowledge, divine illumination, divine ideas, angelic cognition, freedom, and the relation between powers and substance. The result is not fragmentary. It is a highly coherent medieval philosophy of mind whose governing idea is this: created mind is a truth-seeking and good-seeking power rooted in the rational soul, naturally active through sense-based abstraction and free volition, but finally measured by the divine intellect as exemplar and completed by the divine light as the norm of strict truth.

Henry was a secular master at Paris in the last quarter of the thirteenth century, active in the immediate aftermath of the 1277 condemnations. Older historiography often treated him as a merely conservative Augustinian opponent of Aristotelianism; more recent scholarship has substantially revised that picture, emphasizing instead his original synthesis of Augustinian, Aristotelian, and Avicennian elements. His Summa quaestionum ordinariarum famously begins not with being or God but with the question of whether human beings can know anything at all, which already tells us that mind, knowledge, and the conditions of certainty are structurally central to his thought.

1. The central architecture: soul, powers, acts

Henry’s philosophy of mind begins from the rational soul, not from an isolated “mental substance” conceived apart from body, knowledge, will, and God. The human being is an embodied rational animal, but Henry’s hylomorphism differs from Aquinas’s. He is reported by the Stanford entry as holding a kind of human “dimorphism”: the human being alone has two substantial forms, one of which is the rational soul. That is important because it lets Henry preserve both the bodily integrity of human life and the relative independence of the rational soul’s higher powers.

A major point for reconstructing Henry’s philosophy of mind is his account of the soul’s powers. Against Albert and Aquinas, who treat the soul’s powers as accidents really distinct from the soul’s essence, Henry argues that the soul is its powers “through its essence.” The powers add not a separate accidental entity but a relational orientation toward acts: intellect is the soul as ordered to thinking; sight is the soul as ordered to seeing. This relational model also distinguishes powers bound to bodily organs, such as sight, from non-organ-bound powers, such as intellect.

This has large consequences. The mind is not a detachable module housed inside the person. It is the rational soul considered under certain operative relations: as intellect, as will, as capable of truth, as capable of good, as reflexively present to itself. A Henry-inspired philosophy of mind should therefore not begin with a Cartesian theater or a bundle of mental states. It should begin with a substance-power-act structure: the soul is a real living principle; its powers are not external add-ons; acts of knowing, willing, sensing, judging, remembering, and speaking are the actualization of the soul under diverse formal relations.

That also means Henry’s “faculty psychology” is not crude compartmentalism. Faculties are not little agents inside the agent. They are the soul itself under different rational orientations. The soul’s unity is preserved, but mental life remains internally differentiated. This is especially important for intellect and will: they are distinct as powers by relation to distinct acts and objects, yet they are rooted in one rational soul.

2. Sense, abstraction, intellect: the natural basis of human cognition

Henry is not a simple anti-Aristotelian illuminationist. He explicitly holds that human knowledge begins from the senses. The Stanford entry notes that Henry distinguishes sensory apprehension from intellectual judgment and allows that the soul has a natural cognitive dignity: it can know something “from purely natural principles,” without a special divine illumination, at least when knowledge is taken in a broad sense.

The basic movement of human cognition is therefore Aristotelian in structure. Sensation supplies phantasms; the intellect abstracts; the mind forms universal intelligible content; judgment then determines truth or falsity. Henry’s disagreement with more naturalistic Aristotelians does not concern whether sensory experience matters. It concerns whether sense-based abstraction and the created intellect suffice for strict, infallible, essence-grasping truth.

Henry’s account of intelligible species is subtle and cannot be reduced to a simple yes-or-no formula. Some discussions present him as partially rejecting the mediating role of intelligible species: rather than positing an intelligible species as an intermediary object between knower and thing, Henry can say that the phantasm, made immaterial by the agent intellect, impresses the possible intellect. The divine exemplar then acts upon the already formed mental word, refining it into a more perfect word. The Stanford entry therefore describes him not as anti-representationalist but as trying to eliminate unnecessary mediating entities.

At the same time, recent work on Henry’s self-cognition notes that in Quodlibet I, qq. 12–13, Henry still says the embodied intellect, being in potency, requires actualization by intelligible species in order to cognize extramental things. The best reconstruction is developmental and contextual: Henry accepts that embodied human cognition needs a mediating actualization from phantasm or species-like intelligible content, but he resists treating the species as an autonomous object that replaces the thing known.

The human mind is thus naturally receptive, abstractive, and judicative. It receives through the senses, abstracts through intellect, and judges through composition and division. But its highest cognitive achievement is not mere representation. It is the grasp of truth, and for Henry truth is not exhausted by correspondence to a mutable created thing.

3. Two levels of truth: natural cognition and strict certainty

Henry’s most famous epistemological thesis is his defense of divine illumination. But this thesis must be formulated carefully. He does not say that ordinary human beings know nothing unless God injects concepts into their minds. He does not say that divine illumination replaces sensation, abstraction, or intellectual judgment. He does not say that illumination gives us a beatific vision of God in this life. What he says is sharper: natural cognition suffices for many cognitive acts, but strict knowledge of the truth of a thing, especially the truth of an essence under an infallible measure, requires a divine exemplar.

Henry distinguishes between knowing something true and knowing truth in the strict, pure, or “sincere” sense. Sensory and intellectual powers can naturally produce true cognition. Yet because both the created object and the created mind are mutable, they cannot by themselves provide the unchangeable measure required for infallible certainty. The mind must in some way be measured by the eternal exemplar in the divine intellect. The Stanford entry describes this as Henry’s doctrine of the “double exemplar”: the created mental exemplar or universal formed by the human mind, and the uncreated exemplar, namely the divine idea. God functions here as ratio cognoscendi, the reason or ground by which something is known, not as the obiectum cognitum, the object directly known.

This is the hinge of Henry’s philosophy of mind. The human intellect has genuine natural power, but the norm of strict truth is not internal to finite psychology. The mind’s act is completed by a relation to the divine exemplar. Divine illumination is therefore not a supernatural database but a certification or stabilization of the created exemplar by the uncreated exemplar. A francophone interpretation of Henry makes the same point: the “skeptic” in Henry is largely a strategic figure, and the doctrine aims to show how knowledge is possible by distinguishing ordinary truth from sincera veritas.

The Cambridge framing is useful here: Henry is the last great defender of divine illumination in the thirteenth century, but he defends it precisely by specifying what kind of knowledge requires special illumination. Thomas Aquinas had already weakened illumination into a general dependence on God’s natural light and providential movement; Scotus later criticizes Henry more sharply and helps push scholastic epistemology toward a more naturalistic account of certainty.

For philosophy of mind, the crucial implication is that Henry offers a form of epistemic externalism avant la lettre, but not the externalism of causal reliability theory. The external norm is metaphysical and theological: a finite mental act counts as strictly true because it is measured by the eternal exemplar. Human cognition is neither autonomous in the modern rationalist sense nor passive in a fideist sense. It is naturally active, but normatively dependent.

4. Mental representation: phantasm, concept, exemplar, mental word

Henry’s account of cognition contains several layers of representation.

First, there is the sensible level: the external object acts through the senses and produces sensory apprehension. Second, there is the phantasm: an image-like content associated with the sensitive soul. Third, there is the intellectual level: the intellect abstracts universal content. Fourth, there is the verbum mentis, the mental word, which is the mind’s formed intelligible expression of what it has understood. Fifth, for strict truth, there is a relation between that created mental content and the divine exemplar.

This structure avoids two opposite reductions. Henry does not reduce cognition to raw sensation; the intellect has its own formal act. But he also does not reduce cognition to free-floating intelligibles detached from sense; all ordinary human cognition begins from the senses. Nor does he reduce truth to subjective mental representation; the mental word must be measured by the thing and, ultimately, by the divine idea.

Henry’s philosophy of mind is therefore representational, but not in the sense that the mind directly knows only private internal images. The representation is not a veil. It is the mind’s formal way of possessing the intelligible content of the thing. Yet, because the created representation is fallible and mutable, it requires the divine exemplar for the highest mode of truth.

This helps explain why Henry can sound at once Aristotelian, Augustinian, and Avicennian. From Aristotle he takes the sense-to-intellect architecture. From Augustine he takes the exemplarist and illuminationist account of certainty. From Avicenna he inherits a metaphysical vocabulary in which essences, possibilities, and intentional modes of being can be analyzed with great precision. The result is not eclectic confusion but a layered noetics.

5. Self-knowledge: presence, intuition, reflection

Self-knowledge is one of the most promising areas for extracting a full philosophy of mind from Henry. Recent scholarship treats Henry’s account as involving more than one model. In earlier discussions, the question is whether the mind knows itself through its own essence or through an intelligible species. Later Henry develops what Yanfu Chen calls a “Direct Intuition Model,” according to which the mind directly cognizes its own existence and distinctness through self-presence. He also develops a “Reflection Model,” according to which self-cognition occurs when the mind reflects on prior acts, requiring a universal phantasm. Chen argues that these models are complementary rather than contradictory: Henry recognizes dispositional self-knowing, intuitive self-thinking, and introspective self-reflection.

This is philosophically important. Henry does not simply say, “the mind is transparent to itself.” Nor does he reduce self-knowledge to inference from behavior or bodily signs. The mind is self-present in a way that permits immediate awareness of its existence and distinctness, but discursive, conceptual, and reflective knowledge of the self requires acts, contents, and intellectual mediation.

A Henry-inspired account of self-consciousness would therefore distinguish at least three levels. First, self-presence: the soul is not hidden from itself in the way an external object is hidden. Second, occurrent self-awareness: the mind can directly recognize itself as existing and as distinct. Third, reflective self-knowledge: the mind can take its own acts as objects of cognition and understand itself conceptually. This is neither Cartesian infallibilism nor empiricist self-opacity. It is a graded account of reflexivity.

It also fits Henry’s broader metaphysics of powers. Since the powers are the soul itself under operative relations, the soul’s awareness of its own acts is not a confrontation with alien internal objects. When the mind reflects on thinking, willing, or judging, it returns upon its own activity. But because human cognition is embodied and phantasm-dependent, even self-reflection in the full conceptual sense is not wholly detached from the conditions of embodied intellect.

6. Will, intellect, and freedom

Henry is often described as a voluntarist, but that label must be used with precision. He does give the will a remarkable primacy. The Stanford entry notes that Henry treats the will as superior to the intellect in habit, act, and object, while also holding that reason has primacy with respect to the ordering of action. The intellect presents the universal and the good; the will moves toward the good and exercises self-motion.

Henry’s will is not a passive appetite dragged along by the intellect’s last judgment. It has virtus ad movendum, a power to move. It can pass from potency to act because it virtually contains its act. The intellect and the presented good are conditions for willing, but they are not sufficient necessitating causes. Henry even insists that the will remains free with respect to the supreme Good, at least in the relevant respect of exercise or self-motion.

Francophone scholarship brings out the same point by emphasizing dominium, mastery. Henry defines the will’s freedom as a kind of control: the will is freer than the intellect because the intellect is constrained by its objects and can be directed by the will. The will can move the intellect to act or to cease acting; the intellect may be spontaneous in some sense, but it is more subject to mastery than the will is.

This does not mean Henry makes the will irrational. He does not deny the principle that nothing is willed unless somehow known. The intellect remains a necessary condition: the will cannot will an end unless that end is in some manner presented as knowable or good. But the intellect is not a sufficient determinant of voluntary action. The will is a self-moving power that can govern attention, inquiry, assent, and practical direction.

The philosophy-of-mind payoff is substantial. Henry’s theory of agency includes something like a medieval account of executive control. The will is not merely desire; it is the rational soul’s power of free orientation toward the good, including the power to direct intellectual activity. Mental life is therefore not only representational but also volitional. Thinking is not just something that happens in us; it is often something we can take up, suspend, redirect, or pursue under the governance of will.

7. God as mind: divine intellect, divine ideas, and the noetic ground of creation

Henry’s comments on the divine mind are not theological decorations. They are structurally indispensable. Human cognition, truth, essence, possibility, and strict certainty all depend on the divine intellect as the source of exemplars.

For Henry, the primary object of divine knowledge is God’s own essence. Creatures are secondary objects of divine knowledge insofar as the divine essence is imitable by them. Divine ideas are not mental pictures in God. They are eternal exemplars grounded in the possible imitability of the divine essence. The divine intellect is not passively informed by objects; it actively causes or constitutes its secondary objects as intelligible essences.

Henry’s theory of essences is tied to this divine noetics. Created essences, as objects of divine knowledge, possess esse essentiae, the being of essence, before they possess actual existence through divine will. The Stanford entry describes these essences as entia diminuta, diminished beings, comparable in one respect to contents of thought but crucially different because God’s intellect is not passive and because divine intellection causes its contents. Divine will then actualizes essences into real existence.

This gives Henry a very strong account of divine mind. In human cognition, the mind conforms to the thing and must be certified by the divine exemplar. In divine cognition, by contrast, the divine intellect is not measured by anything outside itself. It knows itself primarily, and in knowing itself it knows the ways in which the divine essence can be imitated by creatures. Divine cognition is therefore productive, exemplary, and constitutive.

There is also a controversial question about the number of divine ideas. Henry at points says that divine ideas are finite and correspond to creatural essences, a position the Stanford entry describes as unusual and partially retracted. This should be handled cautiously. The important philosophical point is not merely arithmetic; it is that Henry resists making divine ideas an indeterminate infinity of arbitrary mental items. They correspond to the order of possible essences under the divine intellect.

On the Trinitarian side, secondary literature describes Henry as treating divine intellect and will as fecund principles: intellect as the natural fecundity by which the Father speaks the Word, and will as the corresponding fecundity of love. Another source summarizes Henry’s view that because God creates by knowledge and love, the personal emanations in God are connected to the basis of creation. This matters for philosophy of mind because the highest case of mind is not bare cognition but self-knowing and self-loving fecundity.

The divine mind, then, is the limiting case by which Henry understands all finite mind. Human thought is receptive and measured; angelic thought is created but immaterial; divine thought is self-grounded, exemplary, and productive. God is not merely another mind in the hierarchy. God is the source of the intelligibility by which any created mind can know.

8. God as first known: not ontologism, but analogical priority

Henry also has a striking doctrine of God as the first known object, but this must not be mistaken for the claim that human beings directly and evidently know the divine essence. According to the Stanford entry, God is first known not by evident vision but through absolute indeterminacy. More indeterminate being is grasped prior to determinate being; in conceiving determinate being, we already conceive indeterminate being in a more primary way. God is last in rational, explicit, demonstrative knowledge, but first in a natural and confused way.

This doctrine is tied to Henry’s anti-univocal account of being. Being is not common to God and creatures in the same univocal way. Predication is analogical: “being” primarily refers to God and derivatively to creatures. For Henry, a univocal concept of being common to God and creature is not a neutral metaphysical tool; it is at best a temptation or weakness of the finite intellect.

The mental significance is considerable. Henry’s theory of concepts is not flat. Some concepts are analogical, hierarchical, and ordered toward a first term that cannot be grasped in the same way as finite objects. The mind’s most general concepts are not merely abstractions from created things; they carry a confused orientation toward God as the first and highest instance of being and good. This is another reason Henry’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from his theology.

9. Angelic minds: non-embodied intellect, communication, and mental privacy

Henry’s angelology is philosophically rich because angels function as a test case for mind without human embodiment. They are not divine, because they are created; but they are not human, because their cognition is not sense-dependent in the human way. They allow Henry to ask: what is thought without phantasm? What is communication without vocal sound? What is privacy without bodily opacity?

Tiziana Suarez-Nani’s francophone study of secrecy and transparency in Henry and Scotus is especially useful here. She shows that Henry shares the view that angels have universal knowledge and that angelic language manifests mental contents. Angelic speech is not sound traveling through air; it is the showing of a concept to another intellect. Henry can identify understanding and speaking in spiritual creatures in reality, distinguishing them only by reason: thinking grasps a content, while speaking is the same content as addressed or shown to an interlocutor who lacks it.

This is a remarkable theory of language. A human word is an external sign of an inner concept. An angelic word is closer to the concept itself made available to another mind. Suarez-Nani reports Henry’s distinction between verbum mentis and verbum locutionis: the mental word is universal, while the word of speech is the mental content as communicatively displayed. Angelic speech is thus a kind of intentional disclosure.

Henry also distinguishes natural angelic knowledge from supernatural or revealed knowledge. Natural angelic knowledge, concerning universal quidditative contents, is broadly transparent and public among angels. Supernatural knowledge, especially of future singulars depending on the divine will, is not naturally possessed by all and may need to be communicated. This means Henry does not simply abolish privacy in angels; rather, he relocates privacy. The basic problem is not bodily concealment but asymmetry of cognitive possession.

Scotus later resists this transparency model by emphasizing secrecy, singularity, and the irreducibility of one subject’s mental acts to another’s view. Suarez-Nani reads Scotus as developing a more opaque account of mental privacy against the more transparent angelic communication models of Henry, Thomas, and Giles.

For philosophy of mind, Henry’s angelology gives us a non-human model of intentionality and communication. Angelic language is not conventional sound but the directed manifestation of intelligible content. Angelic mind is thus a pure case of intellectual possession and communicative disclosure. It shows that Henry’s account of mind is not limited to embodied psychology; it is a general theory of created intellectual life, with human and angelic modes.

10. Time, numbering, and the soul’s formal contribution

Henry’s philosophy of mind also touches temporal cognition. The Stanford entry notes that, for Henry, time has a material aspect in the continuity of motion and a formal aspect in the soul’s numbering activity. This is not a fully Augustinian theory of inner time-consciousness, but it does show that Henry assigns the soul a formal role in temporal articulation.

This fits the broader picture. The mind does not create the world’s motion, but it gives formal cognitive articulation to temporal succession by numbering. Henry’s soul is not merely a passive recorder of events; it contributes order, measure, and intelligible discrimination. That is consonant with his general account of cognition: created objects act upon us, but the mind’s formal activity is required for knowledge.

11. A systematic extraction: Henry’s philosophy of mind in theses

Henry’s reconstructed philosophy of mind can be stated in a series of connected theses.

First, mind is the rational soul under its intellectual and volitional powers. It is not a Cartesian ego detached from embodiment, but neither is it reducible to bodily organization. The rational soul is the principle of higher cognition and free willing, and its powers are relational orientations of the soul itself.

Second, human cognition is naturally embodied. It begins with sense, phantasm, and abstraction. The human intellect is not angelic; it does not ordinarily possess intelligibles without dependence on the sensible world. Henry’s illuminationism must therefore be read together with his Aristotelian psychology of cognition, not against it.

Third, representation is real but not veil-like. Mental words, concepts, phantasms, and exemplars are not private screens that block access to things. They are the forms under which the mind intellectually possesses what is known. Henry economizes representational intermediaries where possible, but he does not abandon the idea that cognition has internal formal content.

Fourth, truth is normative before it is psychological. A mental act can be psychologically complete and still lack the highest mode of certainty. Strict truth requires conformity not only to the created thing but to the eternal exemplar. The mind’s norm lies beyond the mind.

Fifth, divine illumination is a theory of certainty, not a replacement for cognition. Illumination does not hand the mind ready-made concepts. It certifies or perfects the created exemplar by reference to the uncreated exemplar. This is why Henry can affirm both natural cognition and the need for divine light.

Sixth, self-knowledge is layered. The mind has self-presence and can directly recognize its own existence and distinction, but reflective self-knowledge requires acts and conceptual mediation. Henry is neither a simple transparency theorist nor a simple inferentialist.

Seventh, the will is a self-moving rational power. It is conditioned by intellect but not necessitated by intellect. It can direct attention, inquiry, and action. Henry’s voluntarism is not irrationalism; it is a theory of free rational agency.

Eighth, the mind is ordered toward both truth and good. Intellect and will are not competing machines. The intellect presents and judges; the will loves, chooses, commands, and directs. The rational soul’s full life is cognitive-affective-volitional.

Ninth, created mind exists within a hierarchy of minds. Human minds are embodied and phantasm-dependent; angelic minds are created but immaterial; the divine mind is uncreated, self-knowing, exemplary, and productive. This hierarchy is not ornamental. It determines Henry’s understanding of cognition, truth, language, and agency.

Tenth, the divine mind is the metaphysical ground of intelligibility. Divine ideas are not optional theological additions. They are the eternal exemplars by which essences are constituted as intelligible and by which human knowledge can achieve strict truth.

Eleventh, angelic minds reveal the communicative structure of thought. Henry’s account of angelic speech shows that language is fundamentally the manifestation of intelligible content to another intellect. Human vocal speech is a bodily mode of this deeper intentional structure.

Twelfth, Henry’s philosophy of mind is anti-naturalistic but not anti-natural. He rejects the sufficiency of created natural powers for strict certainty, but he does not deny those powers. Nature is real, active, and dignified; it is simply not self-grounding.

12. Comparison with Aquinas

Henry and Aquinas share much: both accept a broadly Aristotelian framework of sense, phantasm, abstraction, agent intellect, possible intellect, and rational appetite. Both reject skepticism. Both think human cognition depends ultimately on God. But they diverge on several decisive points.

Aquinas tends to naturalize illumination. For him, the divine light is principally the natural intellectual light given to the human mind as part of its created structure; ordinary knowledge does not require a special new illumination beyond God’s general causality and conservation. Henry insists that strict knowledge of truth requires a special divine illumination, because only the eternal exemplar can secure infallible certainty.

Aquinas holds that the soul’s powers are accidents really distinct from the soul’s essence. Henry rejects that stronger real distinction and gives a relational account: the soul is its powers through its essence, and the powers add relations to acts.

Aquinas is also more intellectualist in moral psychology, although his own theory is complex and evolved. Henry gives the will a stronger self-moving power and a more robust mastery over intellectual exercise. He therefore develops a more volitional account of agency, attention, and freedom.

Finally, Aquinas’s metaphysics of the human being is famously committed to the unity of substantial form. Henry’s human dimorphism gives the rational soul a different metaphysical placement, one that can make the higher powers appear more strongly marked off from the bodily form while still preserving embodiment.

13. Comparison with Scotus

Scotus is the critical successor who forces Henry’s positions into sharper relief. On illumination, Scotus argues that Henry’s appeal to divine light does not solve the problem of certainty in the way Henry wants. If human cognition is fallible, Scotus thinks illumination does not automatically remove the relevant fallibility; the account must instead locate certainty more securely within natural cognitive powers, first principles, and the structure of evidence. The Stanford entry on divine illumination presents Scotus’s critique as a major reason why the theory declined after Henry.

On being, Scotus’s doctrine of univocity stands against Henry’s analogical account. Henry thinks a univocal concept common to God and creatures is a confusion or temptation; Scotus thinks univocity is necessary for metaphysics and natural theology. This is not merely a metaphysical dispute. It affects philosophy of mind because it concerns the structure and reach of our most general concepts.

On mental privacy and angelic communication, Scotus also moves away from Henry’s transparency model. Suarez-Nani reads Scotus as emphasizing secrecy, opacity, and the privacy of intellectual acts against Henry’s more communicative conception of angelic mental content.

On freedom, Scotus and Henry both strengthen the will against deterministic intellectualism, but they do so differently. Henry’s will is strongly self-moving and exercises mastery; Scotus criticizes some Henrician formulations while developing his own account of will, contingency, and synchronic freedom. The Spanish- and French-language reception has continued to treat the Henry–Scotus debate over intellect, will, and freedom as central to late-thirteenth-century philosophy of mind and action.

14. Interpretive cautions

The first mistake to avoid is saying that Henry denies natural knowledge. He does not. He says that all human cognition begins from sense and that the human soul has natural cognitive powers. The special role of divine illumination concerns strict truth and certainty, not every act of apprehension or ordinary judgment.

The second mistake is treating divine illumination as if God were directly seen as an object in every act of knowledge. Henry explicitly distinguishes God as ratio cognoscendi from God as obiectum cognitum. The divine exemplar grounds and certifies; it is not ordinarily the directly known object in human scientific cognition.

The third mistake is reducing Henry’s voluntarism to irrationalism. The will is superior and self-moving, but it still requires intellectual presentation of the good. Henry’s view is better understood as a theory of rational freedom, executive control, and volitional self-motion.

The fourth mistake is treating God and angels as irrelevant theological appendices. In Henry, the divine mind grounds essence, truth, possibility, and certainty. Angelic minds clarify non-embodied cognition, intellectual communication, and the metaphysics of mental privacy. Removing them would not modernize Henry’s philosophy of mind; it would mutilate it.

The fifth mistake is translating Henry too quickly into modern categories. “Mind” for Henry is not a private container of mental states. It is the rational soul in act and potency, ordered toward truth and good, embedded in a hierarchy of created and uncreated intellects, and measured by divine exemplarity.

15. Final synthesis

Henry of Ghent’s philosophy of mind can be summarized as an exemplarist, voluntarist, and hierarchical theory of rational life.

It is exemplarist because knowledge is finally measured by divine ideas. The created mind can form concepts and judgments naturally, but strict truth requires the eternal exemplar. It is voluntarist because the will is not merely an appetite following intellectual determination; it is a self-moving rational power with mastery over action and, in some respects, over intellectual exercise. It is hierarchical because mind appears in three principal modes: divine mind as self-knowing and productive exemplar; angelic mind as immaterial created intellect capable of concept-disclosure; and human mind as embodied rational soul dependent on sensation, abstraction, and divine certification.

The deepest Henrician claim is that finite mind is real but not self-sufficient. It can know, judge, reflect, will, love, and command. It has natural dignity. But its truth is not grounded merely in its own acts, and its freedom is not mere indifference. The mind is a created participation in intelligibility and goodness. It is most itself when it receives the world through sense, forms intelligible truth through intellect, directs itself through will, and is measured by the divine mind whose ideas are the ultimate exemplars of all knowable things.
